--- Page 1 ---
Spectral Graph Theoretic Methods for Enhancing
Network Robustness in Robot Localization
Neelkamal Somisetty1, Harsha Nagarajan2,∗, Swaroop Darbha1
Abstract—This paper addresses the optimization of edge-
weighted networks by maximizing algebraic connectivity to
enhance network robustness. Motivated by the need for precise
robot position estimation in cooperative localization and pose-
graph sparsification in Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
(SLAM), the algebraic connectivity maximization problem is
formulated as a Mixed Integer Semi-Definite Program (MISDP),
which is NP-hard. Leveraging spectral graph theoretic methods,
specifically Cheeger’s inequality, this work introduces novel
“Cheeger cuts” to strengthen and efficiently solve medium-scale
MISDPs. Further, a new Mixed Integer Linear Program (MILP)
is developed for efficiently computing Cheeger cuts, implemented
within an outer-approximation algorithm for solving the MISDP.
A greedy k-opt heuristic is also presented, producing high-quality
solutions that serve as valid lower bounds for Cheeger cuts.
Comprehensive numerical analyses demonstrate the efficacy of
strengthened cuts via substantial improvements in run times on
synthetic and realistic robot localization datasets.
I. INTRODUCTION
Localization is pivotal for the autonomy of mobile robots,
enabling precise determination of sensor positions in var-
ious critical domains, including environmental monitoring,
surveillance, mapping in GPS-challenged environments, object
tracking, and geographic routing [1, 2]. A prominent method
in this discipline is Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
(SLAM), wherein robots use exteroceptive sensors (e.g., cam-
eras, lasers, sonars) along with motion data from interoceptive
sensors (e.g., IMUs, encoders) to concurrently construct a map
and determine their location [3]. In applications involving
multiple robots, cooperative localization becomes essential,
relying on the collaborative efforts of robots to share relative
position and motion data for joint state estimation [4, 5]. This
necessity becomes more pronounced in dynamic networks,
where consistent connectivity is not guaranteed, requiring each
robot to rely on odometry and shared relative measurements
for localization [6].
In such networks, ensuring robust connectivity among
communication links is crucial for effectively assessing the
states of robots. Typically, robot networks operate on the
premise that each robot can seamlessly exchange informa-
tion to synchronize its actions with others. However, the
*The authors gratefully acknowledge funding from Triad National Security
LLC under the grant from the DOE NNSA (award no. 89233218CNA000001),
titled “Modeling and Discrete Optimization Algorithms for Robust Complex
Networks” and U.S. DOE’s Laboratory Directed Research & Development
program under the project “20230091ER: Learning to Accelerate Global
Solutions for Non-convex Optimization”.
1Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas A & M University, College
Station, TX, USA. Email: {neelkamal.sept18, dswaroop}@tamu.edu
2,∗(Corresponding author) Applied Mathematics and Plasma Physics (T-
5), Los Alamos National Laboratory, Los Alamos, NM, USA. Email: har-
sha@lanl.gov
inherent network connectivity may be compromised due to
measurement errors, sensor failures, communication delays,
and disturbances. Algebraic connectivity, the second smallest
eigenvalue of the Laplacian matrix, serves as a key metric for
robustness and has garnered attention from both graph theoret-
ical and engineering perspectives, underscoring its importance
in enhancing network resilience [7]. Algebraic connectivity
is used as a maximization objective to sparsify pose-graphs
in SLAM, addressing the escalating memory demands for
observation storage and the computational burden of state
estimation algorithms in long-term navigation [8].
This paper addresses a specific instance of the robust
network synthesis problem, which remains unresolved, and is
significant in localization applications. The challenge involves
finding a subgraph with up to q edges from a weighted
graph to achieve the maximum possible algebraic connectiv-
ity, known as the algebraic connectivity maximization prob-
lem. This problem’s NP-hard classification underscores its
complexity [9]. In prior research [10]–[12], we employed
a mixed-integer semi-definite program (MISDP) framework
using outer-approximation (OA) and cutting planes (cuts)
for obtaining optimal solutions. However, this approach has
computational limitations when identifying optimal spanning
trees in complete graphs exceeding ten nodes, necessitating
strengthening of cuts. Leveraging spectral graph theory, this
paper develops a novel valid cut by utilizing the relationship
between algebraic connectivity and the Cheeger constant [13]
of a weighted graph, thereby enhancing the efficiency of
solving the MISDP. The contributions of this paper are:
• Introducing valid cuts based on the Cheeger constant of a
graph to strengthen MISDP bounds, leading to better run
times for larger networks.
• Developing a novel mixed-integer linear program (MILP) for
efficient computation of the Cheeger constant in undirected
weighted graphs.
• Developing a greedy k-opt heuristic to quickly generate
high-quality solutions for the pose-graph SLAM sparsifica-
tion problem, involving benchmark datasets such as Intel
with 1728 nodes and 400 loop-closure edges.
The paper is organized as follows: Section II reviews
recent findings on maximizing algebraic connectivity in robot
localization. Section III presents the mathematical framework
as an MISDP with connectivity constraints. Section IV in-
troduces a novel Cheeger constant-based cut formulation and
an algorithm using these cuts. Section V discusses a greedy
heuristic for large-scale networks. Sections VI and VII present
numerical results and conclusions, respectively.
arXiv:2409.15506v1  [eess.SY]  23 Sep 2024


--- Page 2 ---
II. RELATED WORK
A. Importance of the algebraic connectivity in localization
The significance of algebraic connectivity spans various
domains, particularly in localization and perception, where it
is crucial for estimation accuracy in cooperative localization
[11, 14], rotation averaging [15], linear SLAM [16], and pose-
graph SLAM [17]. In cooperative vehicle localization with
noisy measurements, the state estimation error of the covari-
ance matrix is inversely related to the norm of the Laplacian
matrix, suggesting that higher algebraic connectivity leads
to reduced estimation errors [12]. Similarly, [15] observed
that algebraic connectivity inversely bounds the Cram´er-Rao
lower bound on the expected mean squared error for rotation
averaging. Recent studies have further emphasized its role in
minimizing worst-case errors of estimators in measurement
graphs for pose-graph SLAM and rotation averaging, linking
higher algebraic connectivity to statistically reduced error [18].
In applications like cooperative vehicle localization with
noisy measurements [12] and pose-graph sparsification for
SLAM [8], the central problem involves selecting q edges from
a predetermined set to form a subgraph with the highest pos-
sible algebraic connectivity. Here, the edge weights, derived
from relative measurements or poses, are non-negative and
reflect the reliability of the corresponding information.
B. Maximizing the algebraic connectivity
The problem of maximizing the algebraic connectivity sub-
ject to cardinality constraints has been explored in various
applications. In [19], authors considered a binary relaxation
of the algebraic connectivity maximization problem, resulting
in weak upper bounds. For sparsifying pose-graphs in SLAM,
the Frank-Wolfe method was implemented on the continuous
semi-definite program (SDP) to identify networks with higher
algebraic connectivity [8, 20]. However, these methods pri-
marily yield heuristic solutions without optimality guarantees.
The literature also includes various heuristic strategies [21]–
[23] aimed at generating sub-optimal solutions for algebraic
connectivity maximization. Despite these advancements, there
exists a gap in methodologies developed to obtain optimal
solutions for maximizing algebraic connectivity in weighted
networks.
III. MATHEMATICAL FORMULATION
This section outlines the mathematical framework for al-
gebraic connectivity maximization, focusing on pose-graph
SLAM and cooperative localization applications.
Notation. In subsequent sections, scalars (vector/matrix
entries) are denoted by lower and upper case letters, while
vectors and matrices are represented in bold font for lower and
upper cases, respectively. The tensor product of two vectors
v1, v2 in the same vector space is expressed as v1 ⊗v2, and
their scalar (dot) product is denoted by v1 · v2. For any two
square symmetric matrices A and B, the notation A ⪰B
signifies that A −B is positive semi-definite (PSD), i.e.,
A −B ⪰0. Here, ei represents the ith column of the identity
matrix In, where n × n denotes the matrix size. Additionally,
1 denotes an n-dimensional vector with all components equal
to 1. For any non-empty set S, |S| indicates its cardinality.
Let (V, E, w) denote a weighted graph or network with at
most one edge between any pair of nodes and no self-loops.
The number of nodes is n = |V |. Each edge {i, j} ∈E
has a positive weight wij > 0 and a binary decision variable
xij ∈{0, 1}, where xij = 1 indicates the edge is selected, and
xij = 0 indicates its exclusion. The matrix x consists of the
decision variables xij. For a subset S ⊂V , δ(S) represents the
set of edges in the cut-set of S, defined as δ(S) = {{i, j} ∈
E | i ∈S, j ∈V \ S}, where V \ S is the complement of S
in the vertex set V .
The Laplacian matrix of the weighted graph is given by:
L(x) =
X
{i,j}∈E
xijwij(ei −ej) ⊗(ei −ej).
Note that L(x) is a symmetric PSD matrix for a given
network x. Let λ1(= 0) ⩽λ2 ⩽λ3 ⩽. . . ⩽λn be
the eigenvalues of L(x) and v1, v2, . . . , vn be the respective
eigenvectors, where λ2 and v2 are known as the algebraic
connectivity and Fiedler vector, respectively.
The basic problem (BP) is formulated as follows:
(BP)
γ∗=
max λ2(L(x)),
s.t.
P
{i,j}∈E xij ⩽q,
x ∈{0, 1}|E|,
(1)
where q is a prespecified positive integer serving as the upper
limit on the number of edges selected. Given this program’s
non-linear and binary nature, it is crucial to reformulate it
into a more computationally feasible structure. The subsequent
discussion presents an equivalent MISDP formulation for BP.
A. Mixed Integer Semi-Definite Program
In the following discussion, we explore two variations of
BP. The first is a broad-based approach that augments a base
graph to achieve the desired number of edges. This is partic-
ularly relevant for sparsifying pose-graphs in SLAM, where
the base graph is often a path graph, representing relative pose
measurements from encoders. Augmentation involves adding
loop closure edges, symbolizing relative pose measurements
from exteroceptive sensors.
The second variant considers weighted spanning trees as
feasible solutions, with the optimal solution (x∗) being a
spanning tree with maximum algebraic connectivity. Spanning
trees, representing minimally connected networks, are perti-
nent to cooperative robot localization. Unlike the first variant,
this formulation aims to construct a graph with n −1 edges
from an empty base graph. However, the algorithms proposed
can be adapted to various network configurations.
To simplify the remaining sections, we introduce a lifted
PSD matrix, W = L(x)−γ(In −e0 ⊗e0), where e0 =
1
√n1,
such that ∥e0∥2 = 1. We then reformulate the BP into the
following MISDP for the first variant:


--- Page 3 ---
γ∗= max γ,
(2a)
s.t. Wa + Wb ⪰0,
(2b)
W a
ii =
X
{i,j}∈Ea
wijxij −γ(n −1)
n
, ∀i ∈V,
(2c)
W a
ij = W a
ji = −wijxij + γ
n, ∀{i, j} ∈Ea,
(2d)
W b
ii =
X
{i,j}∈Eb
wij, ∀i ∈V,
(2e)
W b
ij = W b
ji = −wij, ∀{i, j} ∈Eb,
(2f)
X
{i,j}∈Ea
xij ⩽q,
x ∈{0, 1}|Ea|.
(2g)
In the above formulation, the objective (2a) through constraint
(2f) reflects the algebraic connectivity of the network. The
validity of this formulation can be found in [10]. Here, Ea
denotes the set of edges available for augmentation, while
Eb corresponds to the edges already existing within the base
graph. For the variant focusing on spanning trees, we set
q = n −1 in BP, indicating that the ideal network config-
uration is a sub-graph with n−1 edges, making each network
a spanning tree. Consequently, T represents the exhaustive
set of nn−2 possible spanning trees. Since this variant lacks
an initial base graph, Ea is equivalent to E, encompassing
all potential edges. The resulting MISDP formulation is as
follows:
(F1)
γ∗= max γ,
(3a)
s.t. W ⪰0,
(3b)
Constraints (2c), (2d),
(3c)
x ∈T .
(3d)
Solving the network design problem ((2) or (3)) using
general-purpose MISDP solvers is extremely challenging due
to its computational complexity [9]. In our previous study,
we introduced a simplified approach utilizing a cutting plane-
based outer-approximation procedure. For a comprehensive
explanation of this approach, please refer to [12].
IV. CHEEGER CONSTANT-BASED VALID CUTS
This section present a novel MILP formulation to compute
the Cheeger constant or the Isoperimetric number of a given
weighted graph. Subsequently, an algorithm is introduced
to find a valid Cheeger cut informed by the well-known
Cheeger’s inequality generalized for weighted graphs [13].
These Cheeger cuts, in conjunction with eigenvector cuts, are
utilized for the accelerated iterative refinement of an outer-
approximation (OA) of the MISDP in F1 in (3).
A. MILP formulation for Cheeger constant evaluation
Definition 1. Let G = (V, E, w) be an undirected weighted
graph. The Cheeger ratio for a vertex set S is
ϕ(S) =
P
{i,j}∈δ(S) wij
|S|
.
(4)
Definition 2. For any undirected weighted graph G
=
(V, E, w), the Cheeger constant, also known as the Isoperi-
metric number or the edge expansion, represents the sparsest
normalized cut-set of the graph and is given by:
ϕ(G) =
min
S: 1⩽|S|⩽|V |
2
ϕ(S).
(5)
Note that the Cheeger constant for a weighted graph can also
be defined in terms of its conductance based on the volume
of S [24]. However, we adhere to the definition given in (5).
Computing ϕ(G) in (5) or the associated cut-set is NP-
hard. We propose the following MI quadratically-constrained
problem (MIQCP) to evaluate ϕ(G) (see [25]). To represent a
cut δ(S) that partitions the graph, we introduce a binary vector
z ∈{0, 1}n, where zi = 1 if and only if i ∈S; otherwise
i ∈V \ S. The MIQCP can be written as follows:
ϕ(G) = min ϕ,
(6a)
s.t. ϕ(1 · z) ⩾
X
{i,j}∈E
wij(zi −zj)2,
(6b)
1 ⩽1 · z ⩽
jn
2
k
,
z ∈{0, 1}n.
(6c)
Note that (6) is a mixed-integer convex program with a convex
rotated second-order conic constraint in (6b). This MIQCP
can be solved using state-of-the-art solvers like Gurobi [26];
however, such solvers often do not converge or require large
run times even on small graphs. Moreover, since ϕ(G) is
evaluated repeatedly within the branch-and-cut framework of
Algorithm 1, we propose an exact MILP reformulation that
achieves faster run times on larger graphs:
ϕ(G) = min ϕ,
(7a)
s.t. 1 · c
ϕz ⩾
X
{i,j}∈E
wij(zi + zj −2 bZij),
(7b)
bZij ⩽min{zi, zj}, ∀{i, j} ∈E,
(7c)
bZij ⩾max{0, zi + zj −1}, ∀{i, j} ∈E,
(7d)
c
ϕzi ⩾max{0, (ϕ + ϕ · zi −ϕ)}, ∀i ∈V
(7e)
c
ϕzi ⩽min{ϕ, ϕ · zi}, ∀i ∈V
(7f)
1 ⩽1 · z ⩽
jn
2
k
,
z ∈{0, 1}n.
(7g)
where ϕ is any valid upper bound on the Cheeger constant ϕ of
the graph. Constraint (7b) is derived by introducing auxiliary
variables c
ϕzi and bZij, representing the products ϕzi and zizj,
respectively. Constraints (7c) and (7d) linearize the binary
product zizj over the set of binary points with its convex
hull, while constraints (7e) and (7f) represent the McCormick
linearization of the product of the continuous variable ϕ and
the binary variable zi. These linearizations are exact, ensuring
that the optimal solution to these constraints is also optimal for
formulation (6). Finally, constraint (7g) ensures that the graph
is partitioned into S and V \S, with S containing at least one
node but not exceeding half the total number of nodes in the
graph.


--- Page 4 ---
−1
1
0.5
1
(0, 0)
(1, 1)
(−1, 1)
(zi −zj)
zsq
ij
zsq
ij = (zi −zj)2
zsq
ij = zi + zj −2 cZij
Fig. 1: Convex hull as the feasible region (in red) for every
line {i, j} ∈E.
Remark 1. For each line {i, j} ∈E, with zi, zj ∈{0, 1},
observe that the feasible region of (zi −zj)2 in it’s epigraph
form in constraint (6b) is the region above the dotted blue
line in Fig. 1, labeled as zsq
ij . In contrast, the feasible region
induced by (zi+zj −2 bZij)’s epigraph in constraint (7b) forms
the convex hull within the shaded red region, also labeled
as zsq
ij , a proper subset of the former region. This property,
coupled with the summation of such terms over |E| edges,
partially explains why the MILP in (7) runs significantly faster
on dense graphs. (see VI-B)
B. Implementation of Cheeger cuts for solving MISDP in (3)
Theorem 1. (Cheeger’s Inequality [13]). Let G = (V, E, w)
be a weighted undirected graph and let λ2 be the algebraic
connectivity of graph G. Then
cf · λ2(G) ⩽ϕ(G) ⩽
p
2λ2(G),
where cf = 1/2.
Cheeger’s inequality is a fundamental result in spectral
graph theory that relates the algebraic connectivity λ2(G) of
a graph G to its Cheeger constant ϕ(G) (from section IV-A).
Qualitatively, this implies that λ2(G) is large if and only if
ϕ(G) is large. Leveraging this theorem, we propose the novel
Cheeger cut based on the left side of the inequality that is
valid for the optimal solution of the MISDP in (3).
Lemma 1. (Cheeger Cut) Let bG be the best-known feasible
solution for the MISDP in (3), with λ2( bG) representing its
algebraic connectivity, serving as a lower bound to γ∗. Let
cf > 0 be a constant, referred to as the Cheeger factor. For
any other feasible graph ˜G in the MISDP, let ˜S and ˜V \ ˜S
denote the partitions of the graph corresponding to ϕ( ˜G). If
ϕ( ˜G) < cf · λ2( bG), then the following cut is valid for the
MISDP’s optimal solution (G∗, γ∗):
X
{i,j}∈δ( ˜S)
wijxij ⩾cf · λ2( bG) · | ˜S|
Proof. By Cheeger’s inequality, we know that ϕ(G) ⩾cf ·
λ2(G) for any feasible graph G, where cf = 0.5. In particular,
for the optimal graph G∗, it holds that ϕ(G∗) ⩾cf · λ2(G∗).
Let bG be a sub-optimal, best-known feasible graph for the
MISDP. Since λ2( bG) ≤λ2(G∗), we also have ϕ(G∗) ≥
cf ·λ2( bG). Now, for any sub-optimal graph ˜G obtained within
the OA algorithm 1, if ϕ( ˜G) < cf · λ2( bG), then the following
inequality serves as a valid cut for the optimal graph G∗:
ϕ( ˜G) ⩾cf · λ2( bG), where ϕ( ˜G) =
P
{i,j}∈δ( ˜
S) wijxij
| ˜S|
from
(4). This cut ensures that any sub-optimal ˜G is separated
based on its Cheeger constant relative to λ2( bG), improving the
convergence toward G∗, thus proving the lemma’s claim.
While a Cheeger factor (cf) of 0.5 is applicable based on
Cheeger’s inequality, the Cheeger cut in Lemma (1) remains
valid for any cf > 0.5 as long as it satisfies the inequality
for the family of weighted graphs under consideration. Em-
pirically, we observe that cf > 0.5 maintains the validity of
Cheeger cuts and improves convergence efficiency of Algo-
rithm 1 (see section VI-C). Establishing theoretically tighter
constant-factor bounds on cf is an open problem and a topic
of independent research interest.
Algorithm 1 iteratively refines the outer-approximation
(OA) of the MISDP by incorporating both Cheeger and
eigenvector cuts [11, 12, 27]. The OA procedure solves a
sequence of MILPs, representing polyhedral relaxations of
the PSD constraint (3b) in F1, thus, significantly improving
computational performance. Algorithm 1 solves the MISDP by
progressively narrowing the gap between the upper and lower
bounds to reach an optimal solution.
Algorithm 1 Proposed Outer-Approximation Algorithm
1: Input: Graph (V, E, w), Cheeger factor cf, Relative op-
timality tolerance εopt > 0.
2: Initialization: LB = 0, UB = ∞, G∗= null, λ2( bG) =
Algebraic connectivity of the best-known solution of F1,
Fu = MILP by dropping the PSD constraint (3b) in F1.
3: Solve Fu. Let its optimal solution be (γu, ˜
W, ˜G).
4: G∗←−˜G,
LB ←−λ2( ˜G),
UB ←−γu
5: For
˜G, evaluate the Cheeger constant ϕ( ˜G) and the
corresponding cut-set δ( ˜S) using the MILP in (7).
6: while
UB−LB
UB+10−6 > εopt do
7:
Update Fu with the following linear constraints:
v−· Wv−⩾0,
where v−is the violated eigenvector corresponding to the
smallest (negative) eigenvalue of ˜
W.
8:
if ϕ( ˜G) < cf · λ2( bG) then
9:
P
{i,j}∈δ( ˜S) wijxij ⩾cf · λ2( bG) · | ˜S|,
10:
end if
11:
Solve Fu. Update LB, UB and G∗.
12: end while
13: Return G∗, lower bound LB and upper bound UB.


--- Page 5 ---
V. GREEDY k-OPT HEURISTIC
We now present a greedy heuristic for the algebraic con-
nectivity maximization problem, structured into two stages:
• Initial feasible graph construction: In this phase, an
initial graph is constructed to meet the required number
of edges, providing a preliminary feasible solution for
the MISDP. A pseudo-code of this procedure is given in
Algorithm 2.
• Graph refinement through k-opt procedure: After con-
structing the initial graph, this phase iteratively enhances
the graph’s algebraic connectivity using a k-opt heuristic.
In this process, k edges are added to the graph and an
equivalent number are removed to create an improved
solution with higher algebraic connectivity. Pseudo-code
for this procedure is given in Algorithm 3.
A. Initial feasible graph construction
The strategy for constructing an initial feasible graph de-
pends on the specific algebraic connectivity maximization
problem variant. In the case of sparsifying pose-graphs within
SLAM (as in (2)), the approach involves augmenting the top
q edges to a pre-existing connected base graph. These edges
are selected based on their ranking, determined by the product
wij · (vi −vj)2, where vi is the ith component of the Fiedler
vector of the base graph.
On the other hand, for the variant focusing on spanning
tree construction (as in (3)), the methodology is guided by
empirical insights from [12], which suggest that spanning
trees with higher algebraic connectivity typically have a star-
like topology. This topology features a central node with
the highest degree, with all other nodes no more than two
hops away. The central node is selected by sorting nodes
in descending order based on the cumulative sum of the
weights of all possible edges that can be augmented to each
node. The algorithm then incrementally incorporates nodes
into the graph, ensuring they remain within two hops, thereby
constructing a tree with higher algebraic connectivity.
B. Graph refinement through k-opt procedure
After constructing an initial feasible graph, the next step
is to refine it by generating a new graph within the local
neighborhood of the current feasible solution. The goal is
to find a new feasible solution with an increased algebraic
connectivity. This iterative process continues until no further
improvements in algebraic connectivity can be made. Consider
T1 and T2 as two feasible graphs for the algebraic connectivity
maximization problem. We define T2 as being within the
k-exchange neighborhood of T1 if T2 can be derived by
substituting k edges from T1. During a k-opt exchange, the
objective is to find a new feasible solution within this k-
exchange neighborhood and replace the current solution if the
new one has a higher algebraic connectivity.
The k-opt exchange process involves two key steps: first,
selecting a subset of k edges from the set of augmentable edges
to add to the current graph, and then identifying a different
subset of k edges from the updated graph for removal. This
Algorithm 2 Greedy k-opt heuristic - Initial feasible graph
1: Input: Graph (V, E, w), q.
2: if base graph exists then
3:
Oe ←−Sort edges {i, j} ∈Ea based on the decreas-
ing order of wij(vi −vj)2 values.
4:
Augment top q edges in Oe to the base graph.
5:
T0 ←−Initial feasible graph satisfying constraints
6: else
7:
S[i] ←−sum(w[i, :])
8:
On ←−Sort nodes in the decreasing order of S[i]
9:
Oe ←−Sort edges {i, j} ∈E based on the decreasing
order of wij values
10:
Central node ←−On[1]
11:
Add the first edge from the order Oe to the graph,
ensuring it includes the central node.
12:
T0 ←−Add edges to the graph in the order specified
by Oe, ensuring all nodes remain within two hops of the
central node until a spanning tree is formed.
13: end if
14: Return T0.
approach ensures that the resulting graph remains feasible
while aiming to enhance algebraic connectivity. Given the
numerous possible combinations for selecting k edges for
both addition and removal, the efficiency of this process is
paramount. To address this, we introduce an effective edge
ranking strategy to create a fast algorithm that efficiently
navigates the solution space.
Edges from the augmentable set are prioritized based on the
product wij · (vi −vj)2, where vi is the ith component of the
Fiedler vector of the current graph. To streamline selection
and improve efficiency, only the top m(⩾k) edges from
this list are considered for inclusion in the k-opt exchange,
yielding a subset of k-edge combinations outlined in step 3 of
Algorithm 3. After adding k edges, the edges in the new graph
are reordered according to wij ·(v′
i −v′
j)2, where v′ represents
the Fiedler vector of the updated graph. For removing k edges,
selections are made from the bottom m of this ranked list
(as detailed in step 6 of Algorithm 3), aiming for the most
significant increase in algebraic connectivity. The parameter
m, adjustable based on computational needs, helps develop a
faster greedy algorithm by focusing on a manageable smaller
subset of edge combinations.
VI. COMPUTATIONAL RESULTS
This section highlights the computational performances of
the proposed algorithms via two detailed studies. The first
study demonstrates the efficiency of the introduced Cheeger
cuts in solving the algebraic connectivity maximization prob-
lem. The second study offers a comparative evaluation of the
greedy k-opt heuristic’s performance.


--- Page 6 ---
Algorithm 3 Greedy k-opt heuristic - Graph refinement
1: Input: Graph (V, E, w), T0, k, m.
2: λh
2 ←−λ2(L(T0))
3: Eadd ←−Subset of k-edge combinations (i.e.,
␀m
k

)
4: for each edge combination in Eadd do
5:
Add the k edges to the T0.
6:
Edel ←−Subset of k-edge combinations (i.e.,
␀m
k

)
7:
T1 ←−Feasible graph obtained from deleting the k
edges from an edge combination of Edel
8:
if λ2(L(T0)) > λh
2 then
9:
T0 ←−T1
10:
λh
2 ←−λ2(L(T1))
11:
end if
12: end for
13: Return T0 and λh
2.
A. Computational settings and test cases
The proposed optimization formulations and algorithms
were modeled and implemented using JuMP v1.2.0 [28] in
Julia v1.10.0. Computational analyses utilized Gurobi 11.0.1
[26] as the MILP solver, running on an Apple M2 Max
processor with 32GB of memory (personal laptop). User-
defined eigenvector and Cheeger cuts were integrated within
the branch-and-cut framework via a lazy-cut callback mecha-
nism. All datasets, including the CSAIL and Intel datasets,
along with the implemented algorithms are available in the
open-source Julia/JuMP package “LAPLACIANOPT”1.
B. MILP (7) vs. MIQCP (6) for Cheeger constant evaluation
The comparison between the MILP in (7) and MIQCP in (6)
formulations, both used for evaluating the Cheeger constant
of a graph with randomized weights, across different graph
sizes and sparsity levels (Table I) reveals significant insights.
A k-sparse graph means k% of edges were removed from the
complete graph. As alluded to in Remark 1, the MILP con-
sistently achieved optimality in all runs. In cases where both
formulations reached optimality, the MILP was, on average,
106.39 times faster than the MIQCP, ranging from 8.28 times
faster for 15-node runs to 215.82 times faster for 20-node runs.
The highest speedup observed was 759.04 times (20 nodes, 1.0
sparsity), with the MILP completing in 0.39 seconds versus
MIQCP’s 294.38 seconds. For robot localization datasets,
the non-sparsified graph’s Cheeger constant evaluation in the
MILP took 2.1 seconds for CSAIL and 10.6 seconds for
Intel, while the MIQCP timed out on both datasets. This
substantial speedup is critical for practical applications, where
the cheeger constant of a graph is repeatedly evaluated within
the separation oracle of Algorithm 1’s framework.

TABLE I: Comparison of MILP and MIQCP run times across
different node sizes and sparsity levels where the runs did not
time out. Time Limit was set to 500 sec, with statistics based
on 50 randomized instances.
C. Algorithm 1’s performance in finding optimal trees
As discussed in Section IV, integrating Cheeger cuts with
eigenvector cuts enhances computational efficiency in solv-
ing MISDPs (F1) via OA. Table II compares the average
run times required to reach optimal spanning tree solutions
with Algorithm 1, with and without Cheeger cuts. Here, t1
represents run times without Cheeger cuts, while t2 and t3
indicate run times with Cheeger cuts for factors of 0.8 and
1.0, respectively. Additionally, tc
i specifies the time taken to
compute the Cheeger constant for each factor, highlighting the
efficiency gains from this approach.
TABLE II: Comparison of average run times: Algorithm 1
with versus without Cheeger cuts to achieve optimal spanning
trees across instances with 8, 10, and 12 nodes. Time Limit
was set to 3600 sec.
n
w/o Cheeger cuts
cf = 0.8·ϕ( b
G)
λ2( b
G)
cf =
ϕ( b
G)
λ2( b
G)
t1 (sec)
t2 (sec)
tc
2 (sec)
t3 (sec)
tc
3 (sec)
8
1.4
0.6
0.2
0.3
0.1
10
502.3
24.1
1.0
4.5
0.6
12
Time Limit
1752.2
15.3
160.5
2.5
The run times (t1) for solving 8- and 10-node networks
without Cheeger cuts show a noticeable increase in compu-
tational complexity as the network size grows. Run times for
12-node networks without Cheeger cuts remain unreported,
highlighting the challenges of larger networks and the need for
more efficient optimization strategies. Incorporating Cheeger
cuts significantly reduces run times, especially for 10- and 12-
node instances, with higher Cheeger factors (0.8 to 1.0) further
reducing times. This improvement demonstrates the efficacy
of Cheeger cuts in enhancing the computational efficiency of
Algorithm 1 for larger networks, particularly in cooperative
localization. Nevertheless, achieving optimal solutions for the
MISDP using both eigenvector and Cheeger cuts remains
intractable for larger networks, as encountered in the pose-
graph sparsification problem.

network sizes (n). The gap and run times are averaged over
fifty random instances for each n.

different graph sizes. The left graph displays the algebraic
connectivity, while the right graph illustrates the run times.
D. Performance of the k-opt heuristic
In this section, we evaluate the performance of the k-opt
heuristic, as introduced in Section V, for the two variants
outlined in Section III-A. Fig. 2 illustrates the comparison
of average gap percentages and run times for spanning tree
solutions obtained using 3-opt, 2-opt, and 1-opt heuristics
across different problem sizes. These heuristic solutions serve
as lower bounds, aiding the application of Cheeger cuts in
solving the MISDP for the formulation F1. As the optimal
solutions are unknown, the gaps relative to the 3-opt solutions
are used as a reference for comparison in Fig. 2.
In addition, we present a comparative analysis of the k-
opt heuristic against the MAC algorithm [8] across different
datasets. This comparison includes results on randomly gen-
erated synthetic datasets (Fig. 3) and real-world benchmark
datasets, as shown in Fig. 4 and Table III.
Fig. 3 compares synthetic instances by graph size, number
of loop closure edges, and augmentation budget, evaluating
algebraic connectivity and run times for 1-opt and 2-opt
heuristics against the MAC algorithm [8]. On average, the
proposed greedy heuristics outperform the MAC algorithm
across all network sizes. Notably, the 1-opt heuristic exhibits
comparable run times to MAC’s, whereas 2-opt, albeit slightly
more resource-intensive, achieves better outcomes.
Table III compares the SO(d) orbit distances for networks
sparsified using the 1-opt heuristic and the MAC algorithm [8].
SO(d) orbit distance measures variations in rotational states
between the Maximum-Likelihood Estimator (MLE) of the
sparsified and original problems, calculable via singular value
decomposition. A smaller orbit distance indicates a closer
approximation of the sparsified graph’s MLE, relative to the
original, reflecting a higher-quality solution [8].
This analytical framework highlights the effectiveness of the
k-opt heuristic in generating higher-quality sparsified graphs,
as visually demonstrated in Fig. 4. In the figure, sparsified
networks (in red) produced by both the 1-opt heuristic and the
MAC algorithm [8] are overlaid on the original network (in
blue) from Fig. 4a. A comparison reveals a more pronounced
blue presence in Fig. 4c, indicating that the MAC algorithm’s
sparsified network deviates more from the original graph,
whereas the k-opt heuristic’s sparsification maintains closer
fidelity to the original network structure.
TABLE III: SO(d) orbit distance comparison for benchmark
datasets between the 1-opt heuristic with m = 30 and the
MAC algorithm [8] under various sparsification levels.
Dataset
Edges added (%)
1-opt (m = 30)
MAC
Random
Intel
25
0.193
0.221
0.435
n = 1728
50
0.164
0.171
0.318
|Ea| = 400
75
0.059
0.063
0.155
CSAIL
25
0.161
0.162
0.581
n = 1045
50
0.099
0.102
0.316
|Ea| = 128
75
0.040
0.041
0.044
VII. CONCLUSIONS
This paper studies the optimization of edge-weighted net-
works by maximizing algebraic connectivity, a key factor for
improving network robustness and precision in robotic net-
work localization. Given the NP-hard nature of this problem,
we introduce novel Cheeger cuts to strengthen the mixed-
integer semi-definite program (MISDP) formulation, utilizing
Cheeger’s inequality, which involves repeated evaluation of
the Cheeger constant for given graphs. We propose an MI
linear program (MILP) for efficiently computing the Cheeger
constant, achieving an average speedup of 106.39 times com-
pared to solving the standard MIQCP variant. The integration
of Cheeger cuts with eigenvector cuts significantly enhances
computational efficiency, enabling optimal solution evaluation
for up to 10-node networks and yielding high-quality solu-
tions with reduced computational times for larger networks.
Additionally, we introduce a greedy k-opt heuristic for quickly
sparsifying graphs in cooperative localization and pose-graph
SLAM applications, which also serve as valid lower bounds
within the Cheeger cuts framework, enhancing scalability.


--- Page 8 ---
(a) Original graph
(b) 1-opt heuristic algorithm
(c) MAC algorithm
(d) Random sparsification
Fig. 4: Pose-graph optimization for the CSAIL dataset. The sequence shows: (a) the original graph with all measurements;
(b) 20% edge sparsification using greedy 1-opt heuristic to maximize algebraic connectivity with m = 30; (c) 20% edge
sparsification using the MAC algorithm [8]; and (d) 20% edge sparsification through random edge selection. In (b), (c),
and (d), the sparsified networks (red) are superimposed on the original graph (blue), facilitating a visual comparison of the
sparsification effects.

“Localization systems for wireless sensor networks,” IEEE wireless
Communications, vol. 14, no. 6, pp. 6–12, 2007.
[2] N. Patwari, J. N. Ash, S. Kyperountas, A. O. Hero, R. L. Moses, and
N. S. Correal, “Locating the nodes: cooperative localization in wireless
sensor networks,” IEEE Signal processing magazine, vol. 22, no. 4, pp.
54–69, 2005.
[3] C. Cadena, L. Carlone, H. Carrillo, Y. Latif, D. Scaramuzza, J. Neira,
I. Reid, and J. J. Leonard, “Past, present, and future of simultaneous
localization and mapping: Toward the robust-perception age,” IEEE
Transactions on robotics, vol. 32, no. 6, pp. 1309–1332, 2016.
[4] S. I. Roumeliotis and G. A. Bekey, “Distributed multirobot localization,”
IEEE transactions on robotics and automation, vol. 18, no. 5, pp. 781–
795, 2002.
[5] P.-Y. Lajoie and G. Beltrame, “Swarm-SLAM: Sparse decentralized
collaborative simultaneous localization and mapping framework for
multi-robot systems,” IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters, vol. 9,
no. 1, pp. 475–482, 2023.
[6] K. Y. Leung, T. D. Barfoot, and H. H. Liu, “Decentralized localization
of sparsely-communicating robot networks: A centralized-equivalent
approach,” IEEE Trans. on Robotics, vol. 26, no. 1, pp. 62–77, 2009.

connectivity and graph’s robustness to node and link failures,” in Next
Generation Internet Networks.
IEEE, 2007, pp. 96–102.

sparsification for pose-graph SLAM,” in IEEE/RSJ International Con-
ference on Intelligent Robots and Systems.
IEEE, 2022, pp. 01–08.

NP-hard,” Operations Research Letters, vol. 36, no. 6, pp. 677–679,
2008.

for synthesizing mechanical systems with maximal natural frequencies,”
Nonlinear Analysis: Real World Applications, vol. 13, no. 5, pp. 2154–
2162, 2012.

connectivity of networks for various engineering applications,” in 2015
European Control Conference (ECC).
IEEE, 2015, pp. 1626–1632.
[12] N. Somisetty, H. Nagarajan, and S. Darbha, “Optimal robust network
design: Formulations and algorithms for maximizing algebraic connec-
tivity,” IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems, 2024.

Problems in analysis, vol. 625, no. 195-199, p. 110, 1970.

2014 International Conference on Unmanned Aircraft Systems (ICUAS).
IEEE, 2014, pp. 134–139.

bounds for synchronization of rotations,” Information and Inference: A
Journal of the IMA, vol. 3, no. 1, pp. 1–39, 2014.

and J. P. How, “Reliable graphs for SLAM,” The International Journal
of Robotics Research, vol. 38, no. 2-3, pp. 260–298, 2019.

and optimal design metrics for pose-graph SLAM,” IEEE Transactions
on Robotics, vol. 37, no. 2, pp. 627–641, 2021.

for spectral initialization in rotation averaging and pose-graph SLAM,”
in International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA). IEEE,
2022, pp. 5608–5614.

ings of the 45th IEEE Conference on Decision and Control.
IEEE,
2006, pp. 6605–6611.

measurement sparsification of pose-graph SLAM,” IEEE Robotics and
Automation Letters, vol. 9, no. 1, pp. 723–730, 2023.

a network of unmanned aerial vehicles,” in 9th Annual Computing and
Communication Workshop and Conference.
IEEE, 2019, pp. 0440–
0447.

thesizing robust networks with a diameter constraint,” Mathematical
Problems in Engineering, vol. 2014, 2014.

free space optical networks,” in Military Communications Conference
(MILCOM).
IEEE, 2010, pp. 1857–1862.
